original_value.notes: Generation-based intensity, 2024 data

methodology_notes: Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Generation-based (not consumption-based). Includes lifecycle CO2e. US grid mix: ~40% gas, ~18% coal, ~21% nuclear, ~21% renewables.

original_value.notes: Generation-based intensity, 2024 data

methodology_notes: Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. China grid mix: ~60% coal, ~16% hydro, ~16% wind/solar, ~5% nuclear. Coal dominance drives high intensity.

original_value.notes: Generation-based intensity, 2024 data

methodology_notes: Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. India grid mix: ~75% coal, ~12% renewables, ~3% nuclear. Highest intensity among the 5 countries.

source.name: Ember Global Electricity Review / Fraunhofer ISE

original_value.notes: 2024 estimate based on Ember country profile and Fraunhofer ISE generation data

methodology_notes: Estimated from Ember country profile (2023: 371 g/kWh) adjusted for 2024 renewables exceeding 60% of generation per Fraunhofer ISE. Germany grid mix: ~46% renewables, ~26% coal, ~15% gas, ~12% nuclear phase-out.

original_value.notes: Generation-based intensity, 2024 data

methodology_notes: Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Russia grid mix: ~47% gas, ~20% hydro, ~18% nuclear, ~15% coal. Moderate intensity due to hydro/nuclear share.

original_value.notes: IPCC Table 2.2 default. 56100 kg CO2/TJ = 0.2020 kg CO2/kWh. Add CH4/N2O for CO2e.

methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 2, Ch 2, Table 2.2. Global default — natural gas composition is relatively uniform worldwide (uncertainty range: 54,200-58,300 kg CO2/TJ). NCV/LHV basis. Applied as US factor since IPCC provides a single global default for natural gas.

original_value.notes: IPCC Table 2.2 default, applied globally

methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. Natural gas composition is uniform worldwide. China-specific data not separately published. NCV/LHV basis.

original_value.notes: IPCC Table 2.2 default, applied globally

methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. India does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.

original_value.notes: IPCC Table 2.2 default, applied globally

methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Germany uses UBA national factors operationally, but IPCC default shown here for cross-country consistency.

original_value.notes: IPCC Table 2.2 default, applied globally

methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Russia does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.

original_value.notes: Fleet avg 400 g/mi = 249 g/km, divided by 1.5 occupancy

methodology_notes: EPA on-road fleet average (22.2 mpg, 8,887 g CO2/gallon). Occupancy: 1.5 persons/vehicle (US DOT 2019). On-road fleet includes all ages, not just new sales. Real-world driving.

original_value.notes: ICCT certified 129 g/km (NEDC 2021) + 20% real-world gap = ~155 g/km, divided by 1.5 occupancy

methodology_notes: ICCT new car fleet average 129 g CO2/km (NEDC, 2021) adjusted +20% for real-world gap. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated). China's BEV/PHEV share was ~35% of new sales in 2023, driving rapid fleet improvement. CAUTION: new-sales weighted, on-road fleet average is higher.

original_value.notes: ICCT certified 122 g/km (MIDC FY2018-19) + 20% real-world gap = ~146 g/km, divided by 1.6 occupancy

methodology_notes: ICCT new car fleet average 121.9 g CO2/km (MIDC cycle, FY2018-19) adjusted +20% for real-world driving gap. Occupancy: 1.6 (higher than Western average, estimated). Indian cars are predominantly smaller/lighter than US/EU fleet.

original_value.notes: UBA TREMOD model, all car types average, 1.4 occupancy

methodology_notes: UBA TREMOD model 2022. Includes all car types (petrol 165, diesel 173 g/pkm). Occupancy: 1.4 persons/vehicle (German national average). This is the most transparent national transport emission factor database among the 5 countries.

original_value.notes: Derived from IEA: 8.3 L/100km x 2,310 g CO2/L gasoline = 192 g/km, divided by 1.5 occupancy

methodology_notes: Derived from IEA fleet average fuel consumption (8.3 Lge/100km, 2019). Russia has no fuel economy standards. ~16% above global average per IEA. Post-2019 data unavailable. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated).

original_value.notes: All bus types average (transit + intercity). 177 g/mi = 110 g/km

methodology_notes: CBO 2022 analysis of US bus emissions. All bus types (transit + intercity coach). US transit buses have low average occupancy (~25% capacity), inflating per-pkm. Intercity coaches have higher occupancy. Blended average.

source.name: Academic study (Beijing bus smart card data)

original_value.notes: Beijing city buses, smart card data analysis

methodology_notes: Beijing-specific study using smart card ridership data. Chinese urban buses have higher occupancy than US/EU. CAUTION: Beijing data only, not nationally representative. Tier-2/3 cities may differ significantly. No national aggregate available.

source.name: WRI India-Specific Road Transport Emission Factors

original_value.notes: Intracity bus, excludes BRT and intercity

methodology_notes: WRI 2015 India-specific factors. Extremely low per-pkm because Indian urban buses operate at very high occupancy (60-80+ passengers, often standing room). Per vehicle-km, Indian diesel buses emit comparably to other countries. The low per-pkm is an occupancy effect, not a technology effect.

original_value.notes: Scheduled bus average (diesel 96, electric 72, long-distance 31 g/pkm)

methodology_notes: UBA TREMOD model 2022. Scheduled bus average across urban/suburban routes. Diesel buses dominate at 96 g/pkm. Long-distance coaches are much lower (31 g/pkm) due to higher occupancy. German average occupancy embedded in TREMOD calculations.

original_value.notes: IPCC regional default for Eastern Europe/CIS. Derived from diesel bus fuel consumption (~32 L/100 vkm) and estimated occupancy (~27 passengers).

methodology_notes: No Russia-specific bus emission factor exists in any publicly available source. This is an IPCC regional default for Eastern Europe/CIS, derived from typical diesel bus fuel consumption (30-35 L/100 vkm) and estimated average occupancy. Russia does not systematically report transport emission factors.
